content:
# The Patient Green

She kept it on the windowsill above the kitchen sink, where the afternoon light came in sideways and turned everything amber.

It wasn't much to look at — squat, barrel-shaped, wearing its spines the way an old man wears suspenders: practically, without vanity. She'd bought it at a gas station seven years ago for two dollars and thirty cents. The sticker was still on the plastic pot, soft at the edges now, barely legible.

Her daughter called it depressing. "You can't even touch it, Mom. What's the point of a plant you can't touch?"

But she noticed things about it that her daughter didn't stay long enough to see. The way a new arm had been slowly pushing out from the left side for almost two years — patient, deliberate, like a thought you keep almost having. The way dust collected on its flat top and she'd blow it off gently on Sunday mornings, careful not to get too close.

Once, in March, it bloomed. A single orange flower, improbable and brief, gone within four days. She hadn't told anyone. It felt like something that belonged only to her — a small, thorned thing that had decided, after years of simply enduring the light, to make something beautiful anyway.

She refilled her coffee and stood there a moment, watching it do nothing, which was, she had come to understand, its own kind of work.

# The Patient Green

She kept it on the windowsill above the kitchen sink, where the afternoon light came in sideways and turned everything amber.

It wasn't much to look at — squat, barrel-shaped, wearing its spines the way an old man wears suspenders: practically, without vanity. She'd bought it at a gas station seven years ago for two dollars and thirty cents. The sticker was still on the plastic pot, soft at the edges now, barely legible.

But she noticed things about it that her daughter didn't stay long enough to see. The way a new arm had been slowly pushing out from the left side for almost two years — patient, deliberate, like a thought you keep almost having. The way dust collected on its flat top and she'd blow it off gently on Sunday mornings, careful not to get too close.

Once, in March, it bloomed. A single orange flower, improbable and brief, gone within four days. She hadn't told anyone. It felt like something that belonged only to her — a small, thorned thing that had decided, after years of simply enduring the light, to make something beautiful anyway.

She refilled her coffee and stood there a moment, watching it do nothing, which was, she had come to understand, its own kind of work.
